title: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (Ruggie 2011) — Principle 31 (effective grievance mechanism criteria)
text: Operational-level grievance mechanisms should be: 1. Legitimate (enabling trust, independent of management). 2. Accessible (known and easy to use). 3. Predictable (clear procedure with time frames). 4. Equitable (affected parties have access to information, advice, expertise needed to engage). 5. Transparent (parties informed about progress, outcomes communicated). 6. Rights-compatible (outcomes accord with internationally recognised human rights). 7. A source of continuous learning. 8. Based on engagement and dialogue.

title: UNGPs Principle 17 (human rights due diligence)
text: To identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for how they address adverse human rights impacts, business enterprises should carry out human rights due diligence. The process should include assessing actual and potential impacts, integrating and acting upon findings, tracking responses, and communicating how impacts are addressed.

title: OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct (2023 update)
text: Chapters: I. General policies. II. Disclosure. III. Human rights. IV. Employment and industrial relations. V. Environment. VI. Combating bribery, bribe solicitation and extortion. VII. Consumer interests. VIII. Science, technology and innovation. IX. Competition. X. Taxation. Voluntary recommendations; backed by National Contact Points (NCPs) that handle 'specific instances'.

title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Business Conduct (2018)
text: Practical 6-step DD framework: 1. Embed RBC into policies and management systems. 2. Identify and assess adverse impacts in operations, supply chains, business relationships. 3. Cease, prevent or mitigate adverse impacts. 4. Track implementation and results. 5. Communicate how impacts are addressed. 6. Provide for or cooperate in remediation when appropriate.

title: Ethical Trading Initiative Base Code
text: 9 clauses: 1. Employment is freely chosen. 2. Freedom of association + right to collective bargaining respected. 3. Working conditions are safe and hygienic. 4. Child labour shall not be used. 5. Living wages are paid. 6. Working hours are not excessive. 7. No discrimination is practised. 8. Regular employment is provided. 9. No harsh or inhumane treatment is allowed.

title: ILO Convention 29 — Forced Labour Convention (1930)
text: Each Member ratifying the Convention undertakes to suppress the use of forced or compulsory labour in all its forms within the shortest possible period. Forced or compulsory labour means all work or service which is exacted from any person under the menace of any penalty and for which the said person has not offered themselves voluntarily.

title: ILO Convention 105 — Abolition of Forced Labour Convention (1957)
text: Prohibits the use of any form of forced or compulsory labour as a means of political coercion, education, punishment for political views, mobilisation of labour for economic development, labour discipline, punishment for participation in strikes, or racial/social/national/religious discrimination.

title: ILO Convention 138 — Minimum Age Convention (1973)
text: Minimum age for admission to employment shall not be less than the age of completion of compulsory schooling, and in any case not less than 15 years. Minimum age for hazardous work is 18. Developing countries may initially specify 14/16 with progress path.

title: ILO Convention 182 — Worst Forms of Child Labour Convention (1999)
text: Worst forms of child labour: (a) slavery, trafficking, debt bondage, serfdom, forced labour including forced recruitment of children for armed conflict. (b) use, procuring or offering of a child for prostitution, production of pornography. (c) use of a child for illicit activities, in particular trafficking of drugs. (d) work likely to harm the health, safety or morals of children.

title: ISO 37001 — Anti-bribery management systems
text: Specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, reviewing, and improving an anti-bribery management system. Addresses: bribery in the public/private sectors, bribery by/to/of/of-by-on-behalf-of the organisation, directly/indirectly. Risk-based approach: due diligence on personnel, projects, business associates.
